preFix.title: Roast duck legs
preFix.subtitle: Slow-roasted until the fat renders and the skin shatters
preFix.excerpt: Duck legs roasted slowly at 160 degrees C until the fat has fully rendered, then blasted at high heat for a crackling skin. Richer and more relaxed than roasting a whole duck.
preFix.sourceNotes: Slow-roast duck leg method. Widely used in British restaurants and home cooking as an alternative to whole duck.

content.text: Duck legs are an easier and more forgiving roast than a whole duck. The legs have more fat and collagen than the breast and can tolerate longer cooking without drying out. The method is the same as for pork belly: slow heat to render the fat, then a blast to crisp the skin.

items.name: Orange
items.prepNote: sliced, to scatter in the tin

content.text: Render the fat slowly, then blast

Heat the oven to 160 degrees C fan. Pat the duck legs completely dry. Use a skewer or fork to prick the skin all over, especially in the thicker parts of the thigh and around the joint, to help the fat escape. Season generously with salt.
Place skin-side up in a roasting tin with the thyme, garlic, and orange slices (if using) scattered around. Roast for 1 hour 30 minutes until the fat has fully
and the skin has turned from pale pink to golden.
Raise the oven to 220 degrees C fan. Roast for a further 15 to 20 minutes until the skin is deeply golden, crisp, and beginning to blister.
for 10 minutes before serving. Strain and save the rendered fat for roasting potatoes.

items.fix: The skin must be well-pricked before cooking to allow the fat to escape. Add 20 more minutes at 160 degrees C before the blast if the fat layer is still visible and thick.
items.cause: The slow-roast stage was not long enough for the fat to render, or the skin was not pricked.
items.symptom: The skin is still soft and fatty after the blast

items.fix: The 160 degrees C slow roast is the key stage: it should be long and low. If the oven temperature crept up, the legs will dry out. 75 degrees C in the thigh is the minimum temperature needed.
items.cause: Overcooked at too high a temperature throughout.
items.symptom: The legs have dried out and the meat is stringy

items.fix: Pour off most of the rendered fat before raising the temperature. Leave just enough to cover the base of the tin.
items.cause: Too much fat has collected in the tin and is burning at 220 degrees C.
items.symptom: The fat in the base of the tin is smoking badly during the blast

items.fix: Remove the orange slices before raising the temperature to 220 degrees C.
items.cause: The sugars in the orange caramelised and then burnt during the blast.
items.symptom: The orange slices have burnt and are acrid

content.text: Marinate the duck legs in soy sauce, five-spice, and garlic before roasting for an Asian-influenced version. A cherry sauce made from the pan drippings works well. The rendered duck fat from the tin is some of the best roasting fat available.

Duck legs became widely available in British supermarkets in the 1990s as a more affordable alternative to whole duck. They are used in confit (the French method of slow-cooking in their own fat) and in this simpler oven-roast method. For households that want duck on a Sunday without the expense and logistics of a whole bird, individual legs are the practical choice.
